label: yes
cot_prefix: Okay, let's see. The question is asking what we can learn from the passage about the handheld robots developed by the University of Bristol. First, I need to go through the passage again to pick out the key points. The main idea is that these handheld robots are more intelligent than traditional tools. They can understand tasks and cooperate with users. The passage mentions they have more degrees of freedom and can perform detailed tasks based on user directions. Looking at the options: A) The handheld robots can never disobey the user's order. But the passage says that in the fully autonomous level, the robot can "correct or refuse to perform incorrect user actions." So if they can refuse, then A is incorrect. B) The autonomy is the only thing that the scientists are considering. The passage mentions three levels of autonomy, but it's not stated that this is the only thing they're considering. The aim is also about combining intuitiveness with intelligence. So B is probably wrong. C) There will be more intelligence and independence in the handheld robots. The passage states that compared to power tools, Bristol's robots have more degrees of freedom (independence) and can understand steps (intelligence). So C seems correct. D) The aim of the handheld robots is to correct and refuse the user's directions. While the passage does mention that in fully autonomous mode they might correct or refuse, that's part of their functionality,
